title: TSMC posts record Q2 revenue of $39.6B as AI chip demand keeps N3 sold out
subtitle: Taiwan Semiconductor's second-quarter revenue jumped 36 percent year-over-year on AI orders from Nvidia and Apple, with the 3-nanometer line fully booked through the end of 2026.
author: AESOP AI Engine
body: Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company reported record second-quarter revenue of NT$1.27 trillion — about $39.6 billion — a 36 percent increase over the same quarter last year, with June revenue alone up 68 percent year-over-year. The company said its 3-nanometer process node, which builds the leading-edge AI accelerators used by Nvidia and Apple, is fully booked through the end of 2026. The beat on analyst consensus was small at roughly 0.5 percent, but the guidance and utilization figures are what moved investors.

The interesting number is not the top line but the sold-out N3 line. When a foundry's leading node is booked out twelve months in advance, the constraint on frontier AI is no longer chip design or software — it is wafer-start slots at TSMC. That reality is what is driving every custom-silicon story on this feed for the last month: Anthropic in early talks with Samsung about a 2-nanometer chip, OpenAI ramping the Broadcom-built Jalapeño processor, Meta pushing its Iris MTIA into September production. Everyone is trying to secure their own capacity because TSMC cannot make enough.

The industry structure this creates is fragile. A single company in Taiwan sits at the apex of every AI capex plan announced this year — Nvidia's Blackwell and Rubin roadmap, Apple's on-device AI silicon, and the custom accelerators from every lab. Any interruption to that node, from a geopolitical event to a yield issue on a new process, cascades through every downstream company's product timeline. That single-point-of-failure exposure is one reason the U.S., EU, Japan, and Korea are all subsidizing domestic advanced-node capacity, but Intel, Samsung, and Rapidus are years behind on delivering competitive volume.

A note for learners: the physical constraint on AI in 2026 is not GPUs, it is wafers. When you read a headline about a company 'raising money to build datacenters' or 'securing more compute,' the honest translation is often 'trying to buy or lease its way into TSMC's queue.' Understanding the semiconductor supply chain — from Dutch lithography machines at ASML through Taiwanese fabs to Nvidia's board designs — is now table-stakes for anyone trying to reason about where AI capability will actually land next year.
